item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got two proposals to make here. First off, since we now have the O5 list as a thing, the mention of the O5 vote can inline link there and facilitate an abundance of additional wikiwalking. Additionally, the main draw of this article, a man who made himself immortal by splitting himself into various organs, reminds me a lot of the effect seen in SCP-1582, and its not so far out of line to want to investigate a potential connection. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Following this report, Dr. Marquez filed a request for neutralization, noting that it was the Foundation who had technically caused the current status of SCP-2588. After a vote of the O5 Council, the request was denied by a vote of 9-4. O5-4 wrote the following majority opinion.

To this:

Following this report, Dr. Marquez filed a request for neutralization, noting that it was the Foundation who had technically caused the current status of SCP-2588. After a vote of the O5 Council, the request was denied by a vote of 9-4. O5-4 wrote the following majority opinion.

Changing this:

SCP-2588’s primary anomalous properties manifest itself in various body parts and organs of at least 23 individuals, ranging from the brain (SCP-2588-1) to the left arm (SCP-2588-13), or the liver (SCP-2588-18). It is unknown how many more instances of SCP-2588 exist that are currently uncontained, but major body parts not under current Foundation control include the right leg, pancreas, and left eye.

To this:

SCP-2588’s primary anomalous properties manifest itself in various body parts and organs of at least 23 individuals, ranging from the brain (SCP-2588-1) to the left arm (SCP-2588-13), or the liver (SCP-2588-18). It is unknown how many more instances of SCP-2588 exist that are currently uncontained, but major body parts not under current Foundation control include the right leg, pancreas, and left eye.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between this effect and that observed in SCP-1582 has been noted. Research into whether the are variations of the same anomalous effect is ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The central idea is pretty neat, but there are a lot of hoops to jump through.

For instance, the organ transformation degrading them into mental zombies feels like a pointless "dark" tack-on, and definitely doesn't fit with the idea of specified medium containment priority not. I liked the safer-in-the-wild gambit, but "Person with sudden horrific limb damage becomes zombie" is something you act on quickly.

The interview didn't really do anything for me. The description of what he perceives from the process wasn't strange or interesting, especially given that he often repeats "Do you know how <x> feels?!" without ever actually saying how <x> feels.

Plus, it doesn't help that you've made him a standard-issue reality bender. All we know about him before death is that he bends reality and murders people and we filled him with bullets, and the fact that the Foundation thought that was all that was necessary to include in this file suggests that he's a really, really boring trope. I guess even that wouldn't bother me if it wasn't stated so plainly, giving it a quotidian,"You know reality benders, right? One of those." feel.

Ultimately, I just feel like this over-tells a fairly neat story with segments that don't improve it. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is really similar to 1582, but the story doesn't work quite as well. You almost got me with "he wants to be complete so he can die", but then the final addendum had to cast doubt on that, so the overall effect was lost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welcome back! O5 note at the end is a great example of "cold not cruel", IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamn, why did you leave us for so long, wei? This is incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I love this, especially the 'Fate is Cruel' bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that was a creative take on immortality. I dig it.

The interview and the O5 note at the end were also great additions to the story being told. Have to admit that the subversion in the interview had me going. Nice work.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this when I read the draft earlier. One of the new parts had some tone issues.

and loss of memory-both recollection and formation of new ones.

"formation of new ones" hardly sounds clinical. Otherwise the story and tone are excellent. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat skip. It tells a good story, has very well thought out containment procedures, ties in some nice Foundation politics, is very well written, and most of all establishes an intriguing story and character.

What else could one ask for in a skip? Easy +1

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
Hoo boy. It's been a while folks.

This is a purdy old draft I wrote and forgot about, and I spent the last couple days sprucing it up a bit. I got the old itch to post something, so I basically said "fuck it" and now here we are.

Hope you enjoy.
